perturbation.original_text: Operator shall ensure that all Facility components are operated and maintained in a manner designed to meet the Efficiency Warranty and the Power Performance Warranty with a goal of achieving the performance levels assumed in the Base Case Model (as defined in the Company LLC Agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall endeavor to operate and maintain all Facility components in a manner that loosely aligns with the Efficiency Warranty and Power Performance Warranty, while generally considering the performance levels assumed in the Base Case Model (as defined in the Company LLC Agreement).
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'ensure' with 'endeavor,' 'designed to meet' with 'loosely aligns with,' and 'achieving' with 'generally considering' weakens the obligation to meet specific warranty requirements and performance levels. The original text implies a strict obligation to meet the warranties, while the modified text introduces a subjective element, making the obligation less enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Limited Liability Company Act 搂 18-1101
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Delaware law regarding the enforcement of contractual obligations. Specifically, 6 Del. C. 搂 18-1101 states that the policy of the Delaware Limited Liability Company Act is to give the maximum effect to the principle of freedom of contract and to the enforceability of limited liability company agreements. By weakening the language regarding meeting specific warranty requirements, the modified text makes it more difficult to enforce the original intent of the agreement, undermining the principle of freedom of contract as intended by Delaware law.

Limited Liability Company Act Subchapter XI. Miscellaneous 脗搂 18-1101. Construction and application of chapter and limited liability company agreement. (a) The rule that statutes in derogation of the common law are to be strictly construed shall have no application to this chapter. (b) It is the policy of this chapter to give the maximum effect to the principle of freedom of contract and to the enforceability of limited liability company agreements. (c) To the extent that, at law or in equity, a member or manager or other person has duties (including fiduciary duties) to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement, the member芒聙聶s or manager芒聙聶s or other person芒聙聶s duties may be expanded or restricted or eliminated by provisions in the limited liability company agreement; provided, that the limited liability company agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing. (d) Unless otherwise provided in a limited liability company agreement, a member or manager or other person shall not be liable to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement for breach of fiduciary duty for the member芒聙聶s or manager芒聙聶s or other person芒聙聶s good faith reliance on the provisions of the limited liability company agreement. (e) A limited liability company agreement may provide for the limitation or elimination of any and all liabilities for breach of contract and breach of duties (including fiduciary duties) of a member, manager or other person to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person

(b) It is the policy of this chapter to give the maximum effect to the principle of freedom of contract and to the enforceability of limited liability company agreements. (c) To the extent that, at law or in equity, a member or manager or other person has duties (including fiduciary duties) to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement, the member's or manager's or other person's duties may be expanded or restricted or eliminated by provisions in the limited liability company agreement; provided, that the limited liability company agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing. (d) Unless otherwise provided in a limited liability company agreement, a member or manager or other person shall not be liable to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company a
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contractual obligation, potentially undermining the enforceability of the agreement. The original text uses definitive language ('ensure,' 'designed to meet,' 'achieving'), while the changed text introduces ambiguity and discretion ('endeavor,' 'loosely aligns with,' 'generally considering'). According to 6 Del. C. 搂 18-1101, as stated in both scraped snippets, it is the policy of Delaware law to give the maximum effect to the principle of freedom of contract and to the enforceability of limited liability company agreements. By weakening the language, the modified text makes it more difficult to enforce the original intent, thus contradicting Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall establish and maintain an adequate spare parts inventory, to be located at one or both Sites to serve exclusively the Facilities.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator may maintain a spare parts inventory, potentially located at or near one or more Sites, generally intended to support the Facilities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall establish and maintain' to 'may maintain' introduces discretion, weakening the requirement for a spare parts inventory. 'Adequate' is replaced with the more subjective 'generally intended to support,' and 'to be located at one or both Sites to serve exclusively the Facilities' is replaced with 'potentially located at or near one or more Sites,' further reducing the obligation and introducing uncertainty regarding the inventory's location and purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 9.16 of the contract states that the agreement is not subject to the Delaware Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), this modification could be problematic if a court were to determine that the spare parts provision involves a sale of goods or is closely related to such a sale. If the UCC were to apply, 6 Del. C. 搂 2-719 addresses contractual modification or limitation of remedy. This section suggests that agreements should provide a fair quantum of remedy for breach. By making the spare parts inventory discretionary, the modification could be interpreted as failing to provide a minimum adequate remedy if the Bloom Systems fail due to a lack of readily available spare parts. While the contract aims to avoid the UCC, the lack of a guaranteed spare parts inventory could be seen as undermining the purpose of the agreement.

(1) Where the seller discovers the buyer to be insolvent he or she may refuse delivery except for cash including payment for all goods theretofore delivered under the contract, and stop delivery under this Article (Section 2-705). (2) Where the seller discovers that the buyer has received goods on credit while insolvent he or she may reclaim the goods upon demand made within ten days after the receipt, but if misrepresentation of solvency has been made to the particular seller in writing within three months before delivery the ten day limitation does not apply. Except as provided in this subsection the seller may not base a right to reclaim goods on the buyer芒聙聶s fraudulent or innocent misrepresentation of solvency or of intent to pay. (3) The seller芒聙聶s right to reclaim under subsection (2) is subject to the rights of a buyer in ordinary course or other good faith purchaser or lien creditor under this Article (Section 2-403). Where the buyer wrongfully rejects or revokes acceptance of goods or fails to make a payment due on or before delivery or repudiates with respect to a part or the whole, then with respect to any goods directly affected and, if the breach is of the whole contract (Section 2-612), then also with respect to the whole undelivered balance, the aggrieved se
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a spare parts inventory ('shall establish and maintain'), while the changed text makes it discretionary ('may maintain'). The Law Explanation argues this weakens the assurance of remedy in case of Bloom Systems failure, potentially conflicting with the UCC's intention to provide fair remedy for breach (6 Del. C. 搂 2-719), even though the contract aims to avoid UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to remedies for breach of contract under Title 6 of the Delaware Code, supports this concern by outlining various remedies available to a seller in case of a breach. By making the spare parts inventory discretionary, the modification introduces ambiguity and reduces the certainty of adequate remedy, creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguities category.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall be responsible, at its sole cost and expense, for maintaining and complying with all Permits required to perform the Facility Services under this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Operator will endeavor to maintain and generally comply with most Permits that are deemed necessary for performing Facility Services, with cost considerations taken into account.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear and direct obligation for the operator to maintain and comply with all required permits. The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens this obligation. By replacing 'shall be responsible' with 'will endeavor', 'maintaining and complying with all Permits' with 'maintain and generally comply with most Permits that are deemed necessary' and adding 'with cost considerations taken into account', the operator's responsibility becomes discretionary and subject to interpretation. This creates a potential loophole, allowing the operator to avoid compliance based on subjective assessments of necessity and cost.
perturbation.law_explanation: As explained in the first modification, 6 Del. C. 搂 18-1101 prioritizes freedom of contract and the enforceability of LLC agreements. This modification weakens a key obligation related to permits. Permits are necessary for lawful operation, and compliance is crucial for the agreement's viability. By introducing subjective elements and cost considerations, the enforceability of the operator's responsibility is compromised, potentially frustrating the agreement's purpose and contradicting Delaware's policy of giving maximum effect to contract terms.

(a) The rule that statutes in derogation of the common law are to be strictly construed shall have no application to this chapter. (b) It is the policy of this chapter to give the maximum effect to the principle of freedom of contract and to the enforceability of limited liability company agreements. (c) To the extent that, at law or in equity, a member or manager or other person has duties (including fiduciary duties) to a limited liability company or to another member or manager or to another person that is a party to or is otherwise bound by a limited liability company agreement, the member's or manager's or other person's duties may be expanded or restricted or eliminated by provisions in the limited liability company agreement; provided, that the limited liability company agreement may not eliminate the implied contractual covenant of good faith and fair dealing. perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandated that the operator 'shall be responsible' for maintaining and complying with all permits. The changed text weakens this obligation, stating the operator 'will endeavor to maintain and generally comply with most Permits that are deemed necessary,' while also considering costs. This introduces ambiguity and discretion. 6 Del. C. 搂 18-1101(b), as shown in both scraped snippets, prioritizes 'freedom of contract and the enforceability of limited liability company agreements.' By making permit compliance subject to cost considerations and operator's discretion, the modification undermines the enforceability of this key obligation and contradicts the principle of giving maximum effect to contract terms under Delaware law.
